description: Giving amusement is the sphere in which ready wit is the mean.

evidence.note: Section 7 places ready wit in the sphere of giving amusement.

description: Anger is the sphere in which good temper, irascibility, and inirascibility are assessed.

evidence.note: Section 7 explicitly names anger as the relevant sphere for good temper.

description: Boastfulness is the excess opposed to truthfulness.

evidence.note: Section 7 names boastfulness as the exaggerating excess.

description: Bodily pleasures and pains are the domain in which temperance and self-indulgence are assessed.

evidence.note: Section 7 places temperance in relation to pleasures and pains, especially bodily pleasures.

description: Boorishness is the deficiency opposed to ready wit.

evidence.note: Section 7 names boorishness as the deficiency in amusement.

description: Buffoonery is the excess opposed to ready wit.

evidence.note: Section 7 names buffoonery as the excess in amusement.

description: Choice is part of the structure of virtue and a condition of properly virtuous action.

evidence.note: Section 6 defines virtue as a state of character concerned with choice.

description: Courage is the mean with respect to fear and confidence.

evidence.note: Section 7 identifies courage as the mean regarding fear and confidence.

description: Cowardice is the deficiency corresponding to courage in Book II's table of particulars.

evidence.note: Section 7 names the excess in fear and defect in confidence as cowardice.

description: Deficiency is one extreme opposed to the mean in passions and actions.

evidence.note: Section 8 summarizes deficiency as one of the two vices opposed to the mean.

description: Moral virtue aims at the intermediate or mean rather than at excess or deficiency.

evidence.note: Section 9 restates that moral virtue is a mean between two vices.

description: Excess is one extreme opposed to the mean in passions and actions.

evidence.note: Section 8 summarizes excess as one of the two vices opposed to the mean.

description: Faculties are capacities for feeling passions and are distinguished from virtues.

evidence.note: Section 5 defines faculties as capacities for feeling passions.

description: Fear and confidence define the sphere in which courage, rashness, and cowardice are assessed.

evidence.note: Section 7 names fear and confidence as the field in which courage is the mean.

description: Stable character is one of the stated conditions for properly virtuous action.

evidence.note: Section 4 says virtuous action must proceed from a firm and unchangeable character.

description: Friendliness is the mean in pleasant social intercourse.

evidence.note: Section 7 says the pleasant person in the right way is friendly and the mean is friendliness.

description: Giving and taking money define the sphere in which liberality is the mean.

evidence.note: Section 7 explicitly names giving and taking money as liberality's sphere.

description: Good temper is the mean in relation to anger.

evidence.note: Section 7 says the mean with regard to anger may be called good temper.

description: The sphere of magnificence concerns large sums or major expenditure.

evidence.note: Ross says magnificence differs from liberality by dealing with large sums.

description: Habit is the process by which moral virtues are acquired and perfected.

evidence.note: Ross says moral virtue comes about from habit and is perfected by habit.

description: Honour and dishonour define the sphere in which proper pride is the mean.

evidence.note: Section 7 explicitly names honour and dishonour as the relevant sphere.

description: Insensibility is the deficiency corresponding to temperance, though Ross notes it is uncommon.

evidence.note: Section 7 says the deficient type is called insensible.

description: The teachable kind of virtue contrasted with moral virtue at the start of Book II.

evidence.note: Book II explicitly names intellectual virtue beside moral virtue.

description: Irascibility is the excess opposed to good temper.

evidence.note: Section 7 names irascibility as the excess in relation to anger.

description: Knowledge is one of the conditions Aristotle names for properly virtuous action.

evidence.note: Section 4 says the agent must have knowledge.

description: Inirascibility is the deficiency opposed to good temper.

evidence.note: Section 7 names inirascibility as the deficiency in relation to anger.

notes: The repo id follows a mnemonic modern label, but Ross's wording is preserved in primary_label and source_labels.

description: Liberality is the mean in giving and taking money.

evidence.note: Section 7 names liberality as the mean with respect to money.

description: Magnificence is a mean dealing with large sums or major expenditure.

evidence.note: Section 7 introduces magnificence as a mean distinct from liberality.

description: Meanness is the deficiency opposed to liberality.

evidence.note: Section 7 names meanness as the defect corresponding to liberality.

description: The ethical kind of virtue distinguished from intellectual virtue in Book II.

evidence.note: Book II opens by distinguishing moral virtue from intellectual virtue.

description: Nature provides the capacity to receive virtue but does not by itself produce it.

evidence.note: The opening argument contrasts virtue with what arises by nature.

description: Niggardliness is the deficiency opposed to magnificence.

evidence.note: Section 7 names niggardliness as the deficiency in the sphere of magnificence.

description: Obsequiousness is the excessive pleasantness opposed to friendliness.

evidence.note: Section 7 describes the excessive pleasant person as obsequious or flattering.

notes: Ross names person-types here; the repo uses an abstract vice id for graph consistency.

description: Pain is treated alongside pleasure as a key mark and material of moral formation.

evidence.note: Section 3 uses pleasure and pain as signs of states of character.

description: Passions are one of the three kinds of things found in the soul.

evidence.note: Section 5 names passions as one of the three categories in the soul.

description: Pleasure is one of the central affective objects with which moral virtue is concerned.

evidence.note: Section 9 explicitly warns that pleasure is especially hard to judge rightly.

description: The practically wise person supplies the practical standard for determining the mean.

evidence.note: Section 6 says the mean is determined by the principle the practically wise person would use.

description: Prodigality is the excess opposed to liberality.

evidence.note: Section 7 names prodigality as the excess in giving and taking money.

description: Proper pride is the mean in the sphere of honour and dishonour.

evidence.note: Section 7 identifies proper pride as the mean concerning honour.

source_labels: quarrelsome and surly sort of person
description: Quarrelsomeness is the deficiency opposed to friendliness.

evidence.note: Section 7 describes the deficient extreme as quarrelsome and surly.

description: Rashness is the excess corresponding to courage in Book II's table of particulars.

evidence.note: Section 7 names the excess in confidence as rash.

description: The mean is linked to the right rule or rational principle in Book II.

evidence.note: Section 2 introduces the right rule as a common principle for conduct.

description: Mock modesty is the understating deficiency opposed to truthfulness.

evidence.note: Section 7 names mock modesty as the understating extreme.

notes: The repo id uses a modern mnemonic label, but Ross's wording remains the primary label.

description: Self-indulgence is the excess corresponding to temperance.

evidence.note: Section 7 names self-indulgence as the excess opposed to temperance.

description: This domain covers truthful and untruthful self-presentation in words and actions.

evidence.note: Section 7 frames truthfulness within intercourse in words and actions.

description: This domain covers the general social pleasantness in which friendliness is the mean.

evidence.note: Section 7 places friendliness in the pleasantness exhibited in life in general.

description: Virtue is argued to be a state of character rather than a passion or faculty.

evidence.note: Section 5 introduces states of character as the remaining category for virtue.

description: Temperance is the mean with respect to bodily pleasures and pains.

evidence.note: Section 7 identifies temperance as the mean in the sphere of pleasures and pains.

description: Truthfulness is the mean concerning truth in self-presentation.

evidence.note: Section 7 names truthfulness as the mean in the sphere of truth.

description: Undue humility is the deficiency opposed to proper pride.

evidence.note: Section 7 names undue humility as the deficiency in honour and dishonour.

description: Empty vanity is the excess opposed to proper pride.

evidence.note: Section 7 names empty vanity as the excess in honour and dishonour.

source_labels: acts that are in accordance with the virtues
description: Book II treats rightly performed actions as central to becoming and being virtuous.

evidence.note: Section 2 turns the inquiry toward actions and how they ought to be done.

description: Vulgarity is the excess opposed to magnificence in the sphere of large expenditure.

evidence.note: Section 7 names tastelessness and vulgarity as the excess against magnificence.

description: Ready wit is the mean in the sphere of amusement.

evidence.note: Section 7 names ready wit as the mean in giving amusement.
